statement: A C program consists of functions and variables.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The first C program to write is to print "hello, world".
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: To compile a C program, create a file with a .c extension, then compile it with the command "cc filename.c".
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, the function main is special and is where the program begins executing.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A character string or string constant in C is a sequence of characters in double quotes.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The sequence "\n" in a string is C notation for the newline character.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: To include a newline character in a printf argument, use "\n".
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: An escape sequence in C provides a general mechanism for representing hard-to-type or invisible characters.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The program to print a Fahrenheit-Celsius table uses variables, comments, declarations, arithmetic expressions, loops, and formatted output.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, all variables must be declared before they are used.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: C provides several data types, including int, float, char, short, long, and double.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The while loop in C operates by testing a condition, executing the body of the loop if true, and re-testing the condition until it becomes false.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The temperature conversion program uses a while loop to compute and print the Celsius temperature for each Fahrenheit temperature.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, integer division truncates any fractional part.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The code "celsius = 5 * (fahr-32) / 9;" demonstrates how to avoid integer division truncation.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The printf function is not part of the C language, but is a useful function from the standard library of functions that are normally accessible to C programs.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The statement printf("%d\t%d\n", fahr, celsius); causes the values of the two integers fahr and celsius to be printed, with a tab (\t) between them.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Each %construction in the first argument of printf is paired with the corresponding second argument, third argument, etc.; they must match up properly by number and type, or you will get wrong answers.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The behaviour of printf is defined in the ANSI standard, so its properties should be the same with any compiler and library that conforms to the standard.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The code printf("%3d %6d\n", fahr, celsius); prints the first number of each line in a field three digits wide, and the second in a field six digits wide.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The printf conversion specification %3.0f says that a floating-point number is to be printed at least three characters wide, with no decimal point and no fraction digits.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The code #include <stdio.h> /* print Fahrenheit-Celsius table */ main() { float fahr, celsius; ... } is an example of a temperature conversion program using floating-point arithmetic.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: If an arithmetic operator has integer operands, an integer operation is performed. If an arithmetic operator has one floating-point operand and one integer operand, however, the integer will be converted to floating point before the operation is done.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The for statement is a loop, a generalization of the while loop.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The code for (fahr = 0; fahr <= 300; fahr = fahr + 20) printf("%3d %6.1f\n", fahr, (5.0/9.0)*(fahr-32)); is an example of a for loop used to print a temperature conversion table.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A symbolic constant is a name given to a constant value, defined using the #define directive.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The code #define LOWER 0 /* lower limit of table */ #define UPPER 300 /* upper limit */ #define STEP 20 /* step size */ is an example of defining symbolic constants using the #define directive.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The standard library provides several functions for reading or writing one character at a time, of which getchar and putchar are the simplest.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The code #include <stdio.h> /* copy input to output; 1st version */ main() { int c; c = getchar(); while (c != EOF) { putchar(c); c = getchar(); } } is an example of a program that copies its input to its output one character at a time.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: EOF is an integer defined in <stdio.h> that represents the end of a file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, any assignment is an expression and has a value, which is the value of the left-hand side after the assignment.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The parentheses around the assignment in `while ((c = getchar()) != EOF)` are necessary due to operator precedence.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The statement `++nc;` increments the variable `nc` by one.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The `long` data type in C is at least 32 bits.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The `for` statement in C requires a body, which can be an empty statement `;`.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The program `for (nc = 0; getchar() != EOF; ++nc); printf("%ld\n", nc);` counts characters in input.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The `if` statement in C tests a condition and executes a statement if the condition is true.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The statement `if (c == ' ' || c == '\n' || c == '\t') state = OUT;` checks if a character is a blank, newline, or tab.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The program `#include <stdio.h> int main() { int c, nl, nw, nc, state; ... }` counts lines, words, and characters in input.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, a function is equivalent to a subroutine or function in Fortran, or a procedure or function in Pascal.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Array subscripts always start at zero in C.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Chars are just small integers, so char variables and constants are identical to ints in arithmetic expressions.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The decision as to whether a character is a digit, white space, or something else is made with the sequence if (c >= '0' && c <= '9')
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A function definition has this form: return-type function-name(parameter declarations, if any) { declarations statements }
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The value that power computes is returned to main by the return statement.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A function prototype is a declaration that says a function expects certain arguments and returns a certain type of value.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, all function arguments are passed by value.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: int power(int base, int n) { int p; for (p = 1; n > 0; --n) p = p * base; return p; }
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The new syntax of function prototypes makes it much easier for a compiler to detect errors in the number of arguments or their types.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, when an array name is used as an argument, the value passed to the function is the location or address of the beginning of the array.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, every text line has at least one character, even a line containing only a newline has length 1.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: To find the longest line, the program divides into pieces: one piece gets a new line, another saves it, and the rest controls the process.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The getline function reads a line into s and returns the length of the line.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The %s format specification in printf expects the corresponding argument to be a string represented as an array of characters terminated with a '\0'.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, external variables are globally accessible and can be used to communicate data between functions.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, a definition refers to the place where a variable is created or assigned storage, while a declaration refers to places where the nature of the variable is stated but no storage is allocated.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The copy function copies the string from to into the string to.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, the extern keyword is used to declare a variable that is defined outside the current function.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, automatic variables come into existence only when the function is called and disappear when the function is exited.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A variable name in C is made up of letters and digits, with the first character being a letter, and the underscore is considered a letter.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Variable names in C should not begin with an underscore, as library routines often use such names.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In C, upper and lower case letters are distinct, so 'x' and 'X' are two different names.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The standard headers <limits.h> and <float.h> contain symbolic constants for the sizes of various data types in C.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: An integer constant in C is an int, and can be written in decimal, octal, or hexadecimal.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The value of an escape sequence in a character or string constant in C is the numeric value of the character it represents.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: String constants in C can be concatenated at compile time.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The string constant "hello, " "world" is equivalent to "hello, world".
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The strlen function in C returns the length of a string by scanning the string until it finds the null character '\0'.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: An enumeration constant in C is a list of constant integer values, and can be used to associate names with values.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A variable may be initialized in its declaration with an equals sign and an expression.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: If the variable is not automatic, the initialization is done once only, conceptually before the program starts executing, and the initializer must be a constant expression.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: External and static variables are initialized to zero by default.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The const qualifier specifies that a variable's value will not be changed.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The const declaration can also be used with array arguments, to indicate that the function does not change that array: int strlen(const char[]);
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The result is implementation-defined if an attempt is made to change a const.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The binary arithmetic operators are +, -, *, /, and the modulus operator %.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The expression x % y produces the remainder when x is divided by y, and thus is zero when y divides x exactly.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The % operator cannot be applied to a float or double.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Arithmetic operators associate left to right.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The relational operators are >, >=, <, <=, ==, and !=.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Relational operators have lower precedence than arithmetic operators.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The logical operators are && and ||.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Expressions connected by && or || are evaluated left to right, and evaluation stops as soon as the truth or falsehood of the result is known.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The unary negation operator ! converts a non-zero operand into 0, and a zero operand into 1.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The numeric value of a relational or logical expression is 1 if the relation is true, and 0 if the relation is false.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Type conversions occur when an operator has operands of different types.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The only automatic conversions are those that convert a narrower operand into a wider one without losing information.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A char is just a small integer, so chars may be freely used in arithmetic expressions.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Implicit arithmetic conversions work by promoting the lower type to the higher type before the operation proceeds.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The conversion rules are more complicated when unsigned operands are involved.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A cast is a unary operator that forces a type conversion.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The cast operator has the same high precedence as other unary operators.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The increment operator ++ adds 1 to its operand, while the decrement operator -- subtracts 1.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The increment and decrement operators may be used as prefix or postfix operators.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The increment operator ++ can only be applied to variables.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The prefix and postfix increment operators have the same effect when no value is wanted, just the incrementing effect.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function squeeze(s,c) removes all occurrences of the character c from the string s.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The squeeze function uses a loop to iterate over the string s and copy non-c characters to the current j position, then increment j.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The bitwise AND operator & is often used to mask off some set of bits.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The expression n = n & 0177 sets to zero all but the low-order 7 bits of n.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The bitwise OR operator | is used to turn bits on.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The expression x = x | SET_ON sets to one in x the bits that are set to one in SET_ON.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The shift operators << and >> perform left and right shifts of their left operand by the number of bit positions given by the right operand.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The expression x = x & ~077 sets the last six bits of x to zero.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The getbits function returns the n-bit field of x that begins at position p by shifting x to the right and applying a mask.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The assignment operator += is called an assignment operator and is equivalent to expr1 = expr1 + expr2.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The expression i += 2 is equivalent to i = i + 2.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The conditional expression expr1 ? expr2 : expr3 evaluates expr1 first, then expr2 if expr1 is non-zero, or expr3 otherwise.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The expression z = (a > b) ? a : b sets z to the maximum of a and b.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The precedence of the bitwise operators & , ^ , and | falls below == and !=.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The order of evaluation of function arguments is not specified and can produce different results with different compilers.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The statement printf("%d %d\n", ++n, power(2, n)); can produce different results with different compilers.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Writing code that depends on order of evaluation is a bad programming practice in any language.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A side effect is a change to a variable as a by-product of the evaluation of an expression.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: To avoid dependencies on order of evaluation, intermediate results can be stored in temporary variables.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The semicolon is a statement terminator in C, rather than a separator.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The statement if (n > 0) { if (a > b) z = a; } else z = b; demonstrates the use of braces to force proper association in nested if statements.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A compound statement, or block, is a group of declarations and statements surrounded by braces.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The if-else statement is used to express decisions, with the else part being optional.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The binary search function int binsearch(int x, int v[], int n) { ... } demonstrates a three-way decision using if-else statements.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The switch statement is a multi-way decision that tests whether an expression matches one of a number of constant integer values.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The code switch (c) { case '0': case '1': ... case '9': ndigit[c-'0']++; break; ... } demonstrates the use of a switch statement to count digit occurrences.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Falling through cases in a switch statement should be used sparingly and commented.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The while loop statement while (expression) statement is equivalent to expr 1; while (expr 2) { statement }.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A for loop in C consists of three components: expr1, expr2, and expr3, which are expressions that can be assignments, function calls, or relational expressions.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The semicolons in a for loop must remain, even if any of the three parts are omitted.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: If the test (expr2) in a for loop is not present, it is taken as permanently true, resulting in an infinite loop.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The C idiom for processing the first n elements of an array is to use a for loop with the form for (i = 0; i < n; i++)
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function atoi(s) converts a string to its numeric equivalent, and its structure reflects the form of the input: skip white space, get sign, get integer part and convert it.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The comma operator in C is a binary operator that guarantees left-to-right evaluation, and its result is the type and value of the right operand.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Comma operators should be used sparingly, and are most suitable for constructs strongly related to each other, such as in for loops or macros.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The Shell sort algorithm sorts an array of integers by comparing far-apart elements and gradually decreasing the interval between compared elements.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The do-while loop in C tests the termination condition at the bottom, after making each pass through the loop body, and the body is always executed at least once.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function itoa(n, s) converts a number to a character string, generating the string backwards and then reversing it, using a do-while loop to generate the digits in reverse order.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The break statement in C provides an early exit from a loop, causing the innermost enclosing loop or switch to be exited immediately.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function trim(s) removes trailing blanks, tabs, and newlines from the end of a string, using a break statement to exit the loop when the rightmost non-blank character is found.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The continue statement in C causes the next iteration of the enclosing loop to begin, executing the test part in while and do loops, and passing control to the increment step in for loops.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The goto statement in C is a binary operator that transfers control to a labeled statement, and its use is generally discouraged in favor of more structured programming constructs.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A function is a self-contained block of code that performs a specific task.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A function declaration and definition must match in terms of the types of arguments and return values.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: External variables are defined outside of any function and are potentially available to many functions.

statement: The ANSI standard defines library functions precisely, allowing them to exist in compatible form on any system where C exists.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The library implements a simple model of text input and output, where a text stream consists of a sequence of lines, each ending with a newline character.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function getchar() reads one character at a time from the standard input, returning the next input character or EOF when encountering end of file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The program lower converts its input to lower case using getchar() and putchar(): #include <stdio.h> #include <ctype.h> main() { int c; while ((c = getchar()) != EOF) putchar(tolower(c)); return 0; }
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function printf() translates internal values to characters, converting, formatting, and printing its arguments on the standard output under control of the format string.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function minprintf() processes a variable-length argument list in a portable way, using the standard header <stdarg.h> and macros like va_start(), va_arg(), and va_end().
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The minprintf() function is implemented as: #include <stdarg.h> void minprintf(char *fmt, ...) { va_list ap; ... }
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function scanf() is the input analog of printf(), providing many of the same conversion facilities in the opposite direction.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: An input field is defined as a string of non-white space characters.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: scanf will read across boundaries to find its input, since newlines are white space.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Conversion characters indicate the interpretation of the input field.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The rudimentary calculator of Chapter 4 can be written with scanf to do the input conversion: #include <stdio.h> main()  /* rudimentary calculator */ { double sum, v; sum = 0; while (scanf("%lf", &v) == 1) printf("\t%.2f\n", sum += v); return 0; }
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The arguments to scanf and sscanf must be pointers.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: If a file that does not exist is opened for writing or appending, it is created if possible.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The file pointer returned by fopen is used in subsequent reads or writes of the file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: To read or write a file, first open it with fopen, then use getc or putc to read or write the file, and finally close it with fclose.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The cat program can be written to concatenate files: #include <stdio.h> /* cat:  concatenate files, version 1 */ main(int argc, char *argv[]) { FILE *fp; void filecopy(FILE *, FILE *) if (argc == 1) /* no args; copy standard input */ filecopy(stdin, stdout); else while(--argc > 0) if ((fp = fopen(*++argv, "r")) == NULL) { printf("cat: can't open %s\n", *argv); return 1; } else { filecopy(fp, stdout); fclose(fp); } return 0; }
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Output written on stderr normally appears on the screen even if the standard output is redirected.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: To handle errors in a program, use fprintf to write error messages to stderr and exit to terminate the program.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function feof(FILE *) returns non-zero if end of file has occurred on the specified file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: int feof(FILE *fp)
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The standard library provides an input and output routine fgets that is similar to the getline function.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: char *fgets(char *line, int maxline, FILE *fp)
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: fgets reads the next input line (including the newline) from file fp into the character array line; at most maxline-1 characters will be read.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: int fputs(char *line, FILE *fp)
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: fputs writes a string (which need not contain a newline) to a file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The library functions gets and puts are similar to fgets and fputs, but operate on stdin and stdout.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: /* fgets:  get at most n chars from iop */ char *fgets(char *s, int n, FILE *iop) { ... }
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: /* fputs:  put string s on file iop */ int fputs(char *s, FILE *iop) { ... }
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function ungetc pushes the character c back onto file fp, and returns either c, or EOF for an error.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: int ungetc(int c, FILE *fp)
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function system executes the command contained in the character string s, then resumes execution of the current program.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: system("date");
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The functions malloc and calloc obtain blocks of memory dynamically.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: void *malloc(size_t n)
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: void *calloc(size_t n, size_t size)
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function free frees the space pointed to by p, where p was originally obtained by a call to malloc or calloc.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: free(p)
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In the UNIX operating system, all input and output is done by reading or writing files, because all peripheral devices, even keyboard and screen, are files in the file system.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A file descriptor is a small non-negative integer that is used to identify a file instead of its name.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: int file_descriptor = open("file", O_RDONLY);
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The read system call is used to read data from a file descriptor.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: int n_read = read(int fd, char *buf, int n);
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: int n_written = write(int fd, char *buf, int n);
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The return value of read and write system calls is the number of bytes transferred.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A return value of zero bytes from read implies end of file, and -1 indicates an error.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A simple program to copy its input to its output can be written using read and write system calls.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The open system call is used to open a file and return a file descriptor.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: int fd = open(char *name, int flags, int perms);
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The creat system call is used to create a new file or truncate an existing one.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: int fd = creat(char *name, int perms);
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The close system call is used to close a file descriptor and free it for reuse.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: close(int fd);
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The unlink system call is used to remove a file from the file system.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: unlink(char *name);
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The lseek system call is used to move the current position in a file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: long lseek(int fd, long offset, int origin);
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The lseek system call can be used to implement a function to read any number of bytes from any arbitrary place in a file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A file pointer is a pointer to a structure that contains information about a file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The standard library function fopen can be implemented using the open system call and a file structure.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The getc macro can be implemented using the read system call and a file structure.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A directory is a file that contains a list of filenames and some indication of where they are located.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The inode for a file is where all information about the file except its name is kept.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A directory entry generally consists of only two items, the filename and an inode number.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function fsize prints the size of the file by calling stat to get the inode information for the file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: If the file is a directory, fsize calls dirwalk to handle all the files in it.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The function dirwalk opens the directory, loops through the files in it, calling the function on each, then closes the directory and returns.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The st_mode entry in the stat structure contains a set of flags describing the file.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The flag definitions for file type are defined in <sys/types.h>.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The program fsize is a special form of ls that prints the sizes of all files named in its commandline argument list.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The type ino_t is a typedef that describes the index into the inode list.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The system call stat takes a filename and returns all of the information in the inode for that file, or -1 if there is an error.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The structure stat contains information about a file, including its size, mode, and inode number.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The closedir function closes the directory file and frees the space.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The readdir function uses read to read each directory entry and returns a pointer to a static structure containing the inode number and name.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The fsize program illustrates the importance of using standard headers to maintain information and creating system-independent interfaces.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A free block contains a pointer to the next block in the chain, a record of the size of the block, and then the free space itself, with the control information at the beginning called the "header".
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The malloc function rounds up the requested size to the proper number of header-sized units, allocates a block, and returns a pointer to the free space within the block.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The code snippet `typedef long Align; union header { struct { union header *ptr; unsigned size; } s; Align x; };` illustrates how to achieve alignment using a union.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The morecore function obtains storage from the operating system and inserts the additional memory into the arena by calling free.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The free function scans the free list, looking for the place to insert the free block, and combines adjacent blocks to avoid fragmentation.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The code snippet `void *malloc(unsigned nbytes) { ... }` demonstrates how to implement a general-purpose storage allocator.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The use of typedef and union can help control machine dependencies and achieve alignment in storage allocation.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A program consists of one or more translation units stored in files.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The first character of an identifier must be a letter; the underscore _ counts as a letter.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Upper and lower case letters are different in identifiers.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: There are six classes of tokens: identifiers, keywords, constants, string literals, operators, and other separators.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A comment is introduced by the characters /* and terminates with the characters */.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Comments do not nest, and they do not occur within a string or character literals.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The following identifiers are reserved for use as keywords: auto, break, case, char, const, continue, default, do, double, else, enum, extern, float, for, goto, if, int, long, register, return, short, signed, sizeof, static, struct, switch, typedef, union, unsigned, void, volatile, while.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: An integer constant consisting of a sequence of digits is taken to be octal if it begins with 0, decimal otherwise.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The type of an integer constant depends on its form, value, and suffix.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A character constant is a sequence of one or more characters enclosed in single quotes.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A string literal is a sequence of characters surrounded by double quotes.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Adjacent string literals are concatenated into a single string.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The storage class of an object determines its lifetime, and its type determines the meaning of its values.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: There are two storage classes: automatic and static.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Automatic objects are local to a block and are discarded on exit from the block.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Static objects may be local to a block or external to all blocks, but retain their values across exit from and reentry to functions and blocks.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The basic types are: char, int, float, double, and long double.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The void type specifies an empty set of values.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Derived types are constructed from fundamental types using arrays, functions, pointers, structures, and unions.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: An object's type may have additional qualifiers, such as const and volatile.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: An lvalue is an expression referring to an object.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Integral promotion is the process of converting a character, short integer, or integer bit-field to an integer type.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: If an int can represent all the values of the original type, then the value is converted to int; otherwise the value is converted to unsigned int.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The result of converting a floating value to an integral type is undefined if the resulting value cannot be represented in the integral type.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: When a value of floating type is converted to integral type, the fractional part is discarded.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The usual arithmetic conversions bring operands into a common type, which is also the type of the result.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The order of evaluation of expressions is, with certain exceptions, undefined.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The expression E1[E2] is identical to *((E1)+(E2))
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A primary expression is an identifier, constant, string, or expression in parentheses.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The type of an expression of type 'array of T' is altered to 'pointer to T' unless it is in the operand of the unary & operator or of ++,--,sizeof, or as the left operand of an assignment operator or the . operator.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A string literal is a primary expression whose type is originally 'array of char' but is usually modified to 'pointer to char'.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In preparing for the call to a function, a copy is made of each argument; all argument-passing is strictly by value.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The effect of the call is undefined if the number of arguments disagrees with the number of parameters in the definition of the function, or if the type of an argument after promotion disagrees with that of the corresponding parameter.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A postfix expression followed by an arrow (built from -and >) followed by an identifier is a postfix expression.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The first operand expression must be a pointer to a structure or union, and the identifier must name a member of the structure or union.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The result refers to the named member of the structure or union to which the pointer expression points, and the type is the type of the member; the result is an lvalue if the type is not an array type.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The expression E1->MOS is the same as (*E1).MOS
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The operand must be an lvalue; see the discussion of additive operators and assignment for further constraints on the operand and details of the operation.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The unary operator & takes the address of its operand.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The operand of the unary + operator must have arithmetic type, and the result is the value of the operand.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The unary + is new with the ANSI standard, and was added for symmetry with the unary -.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The operands of * and / must have arithmetic type; the operands of % must have integral type.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The result of the + operator is the sum of the operands, and a pointer to an object in an array and a value of any integral type may be added.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The result of the - operator is the difference of the operands, and a value of any integral type may be subtracted from a pointer.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The shift operators << and >> group left-to-right, and each operand must be integral.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The relational operators group left-to-right, but this fact is not useful; a<b<c is parsed as (a<b)<c, and evaluates to either 0 or 1.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The usual arithmetic conversions are performed on arithmetic operands, and pointers to objects of the same type may be compared.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The result of the == operator is 1 if the operands are equal, and 0 otherwise, and the result of the != operator is 1 if the operands are not equal, and 0 otherwise.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The && operator groups left-to-right, and returns 1 if both its operands compare unequal to zero, 0 otherwise.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The conditional operator ? : is a ternary operator that evaluates to the value of the second or third operand, depending on the value of the first operand.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The expression a<b ? c : d evaluates to c if a is less than b, and d otherwise.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In the type comparison for pointers, any type qualifiers in the type to which the pointer points are insignificant.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: An assignment expression has the form: unary-expression assignment-operator assignment-expression
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: All assignment operators require an lvalue as left operand, and the lvalue must be modifiable.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: The type of an assignment expression is that of its left operand.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: In the simple assignment with =, the value of the expression replaces that of the object referred to by the lvalue.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: A constant expression is an expression restricted to a subset of operators.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Constant expressions may not contain assignments, increment or decrement operators, function calls, or comma operators.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Declarations specify the interpretation given to each identifier; they do not necessarily reserve storage associated with the identifier.
source_title: The C Programming Language 2nd Edition - B. W. Kernighan, D. M. Ritchie

statement: Declarations have the form: declaration-specifiers init-declarator-list opt;

statement: C is a programming language that allows a software engineer to efficiently communicate with a computer.
C is one of the most widely used languages in the world and is fairly stable.
C++ is an improved C language that adds a number of new features to the C language, the most important of which is the class.
Classes facilitate code reuse through object-oriented design (OOD).
English is a lousy language when it comes to writing exact instructions due to its ambiguity and imprecision.
Machine language is a series of numbers that can be directly input into a computer.
Assembly language is a simple language in which each word stands for a single instruction.
A typical assembly-language program is translated into machine code using an assembler.
Higher-level languages have been devised to let programmers write in a medium that is easy for them to understand and precise enough for the computer to understand.
C was designed with one goal in mind: writing operating systems, and it quickly became one of the most popular programming languages in the world.
C's popularity is due to two major factors: the language doesn't get in the way of the programmer, and a portable C compiler was widely available.
Java is a language based on C++ that was designed to be 'C++ with the bugs fixed'.
int total;     /* Total number accounts */
A variable is a name given to a location in memory where a value can be stored.
int balance[100]; /* Balance (in cents) for all 100 accounts */
struct rectangle { int width; int height; color_type color; fill_type fill; };
The compiler translates high-level language into low-level machine code.
An assignment statement is used to compute and store the value of an arithmetic expression.
area = (base * height) / 2.0; /* Compute area of triangle */
Control statements, such as if and switch, enable the computer to make simple decisions.
Looping statements, such as while and for, allow statements to be repeated.
A library is a collection of reusable functions that can be linked into a program.
To learn programming, write programs and debug them to gain experience.
Commenting code helps organize thoughts and makes code more readable.
A program starts as an idea, is written in a high-level language, and undergoes transformations to become an executable program.
A source file contains the source code written by a programmer.
A compiler transforms source code into an object file, which is then linked with libraries to produce an executable program.
An Integrated Development Environment (IDE) provides a convenient package of tools for programming, including an editor, compiler, and debugger.
A standalone or command-line compiler is a type of compiler that is operated in a batch mode from the command line.
To create a program using a command-line compiler, first create a separate directory for the program.
To create a directory called hello on UNIX, use the command: % mkdir hello
To create a directory called HELLO on MS-DOS, use the command: C:> MKDIR HELLO
To create a program, start by creating a text file using a text editor.
The following is an example of a C program in source form: #include <stdio.h> int main() { printf("Hello World\n"); return (0); }
To compile a program using the Free Software Foundation's gcc compiler, use the command: % gcc -g -Wall -ohello hello.c
To run a compiled program, type the name of the program at the command line.
To run a program called hello on UNIX, use the command: % hello
Most UNIX systems use command-line compilers, while almost every compiler for MS-DOS/Windows contains an IDE.
Programming in Windows is more complex than programming in MS-DOS.
Select the Options|Compiler|Code Generation menu item to change the memory model to Large
Select the Options|Compiler|Entry/Exit menu item and turn on "Test stack overflow"
Select the Options|Compiler|Messages|Display menu item and select All to display all warning messages
Press the INSERT key to add a file to the project
Press ENTER to edit the selected file
Select the Run|Run menu item to execute the program
After the program runs, control returns to the IDE and the program output is not visible
Select the Window|User menu item to switch to the user screen and view the program output
Select the Project|New Project menu item to create a project for the program.
Fill in the Project Path and Name: blank with c:\\hello\\hello.ide and select EasyWin(.exe) as the Target Type.
Click on the Advanced button to bring up the Advanced Options dialog and clear the .rc and .def items and set the .c Node items.
Press ALT-F10 to bring up the node submenu and select the Edit node attributes menu item.
Select the Options|Project Options menu item, turn on the Test stack overflow option, and click on OK to save these options.
C:> mkdir \\HELLO
Select the Project|New menu item to bring up the New Project dialog
The Project Name should be filled in with "\\hello\\hello.mak" and the Project Type should be set to QuickWin application (.EXE)
Click on the Add button to add the source file to the project and then click on Close
Select the Options|Project Options menu item to bring up the Project Options dialog
The Warning Level should be set to 4 under the Custom Options menu item
The Memory Model should be set to Large
Use the File|Save As menu item to save the file under the name hello.c.
Use the Project|Build menu item to compile the program.
Use the Debug|Go menu item to start the program.
Most UNIX systems have an online documentation system called the manpages.
Use the command "man subject" to get information about a particular subject.
Use the command "man -k keyword" to search for a keyword in the manpages.
IDEs such as Turbo C++, Borland C++, and Microsoft C++ have a Help menu item.
To create a new project in Turbo C++, use the options: Window|Close All, Options|Compiler|Code Generation, and Project|Open.
To create a new project in Borland C++, use the options: Window|Close All, Project|New Project, and Click on Advanced button.
To create a new project in Microsoft Visual C++, use the options: Window|Close All, Project|New, and Set up project.
Most software is built on existing software.
According to Datamation, the amount of time spent on maintenance is skyrocketing.
A program serves two purposes: it presents the computer with a set of instructions, and it provides the programmer with a clear, easy-to-read description of what the program does.
Comments in C start with a slash asterisk (/*) and end with an asterisk slash (*/).
Example 3-1 is an improved version of Example 2-1, demonstrating a well-commented program in C.

To write a program, you must have a clear idea of what you are going to do, and one of the best ways to organize your thoughts is to write them down in a language that is clear and easy to understand.
Make your program as clear, concise, and simple as possible.
Poor programming style can lead to difficult maintenance, upgrades, and debugging of existing code.
Example 2-1 contains a glaring error that many programmers still make, and that causes more trouble than any other problem: the program contains no comments.
A well-commented, simple program is a work of art.
Comments should explain everything the programmer needs to know about the program, but no more.
The actual code for your program consists of two parts: variables and executable instructions.
int account_number;      /* Index for account table */
Units are very important in programming to ensure clarity and understandability.

A good compiler will generate the same machine code for two different versions of the same program, as long as they are functionally equivalent.
Computer scientists have devised many programming styles, including structured programming, top-down programming, goto-less programming, and object-oriented design (OOD).
In order to make programs easier to understand, most programmers indent their programs.
The amount of indentation is left to the programmer, but consistency is more important than the size of the indentation.
A single function should not be longer than two or three pages.
Comments serve two purposes: to help the programmer follow the code and to help the programmer remember what they did.
Variables are the basic building blocks of a program.
int main() { ... return (0); } is the basic structure of a one-function program in C.
The line return(0); is used to tell the operating system that the program exited normally.
/* Swap the two corners */ /* Swap X coordinate */ temp = box_x1; box_x1 = box_x2; box_x2 = temp; /* Swap Y coordinate */ temp = box_y1; box_y1 = box_y2; box_y2 = temp;
Comments are ignored by the computer.
A program should be concise and easy to read, serving as both a set of computer instructions and a reference work describing the algorithms and data used inside it.
A library routine is a C procedure or function that has been written and put into a library or collection of useful functions.
The standard function printf is used to output our message, for example: printf("Hello World\n");
Expressions are used to specify simple computations.
The five simple operators in C are: *, /, +, -, and %.
To calculate the value of an expression, follow the order of operations: parentheses, multiplication, division, modulus, addition, and subtraction.
The expression (1 + 2) * 4 yields 12, while the expression 1 + 2 * 4 yields 9.
A variable is identified by a variable name and has a variable type that tells C how the variable is going to be used and what kind of numbers it can hold.
Variable names must start with a letter or underscore and can be followed by any number of letters, digits, or underscores.
Uppercase and lowercase letters are distinct in C variable names.
The following are valid variable names: average, pi, number_of_students.
Variable names cannot be the same as reserved words in C, such as int, while, for, and float.
A variable declaration serves three purposes: defines the name of the variable, defines the type of the variable, and gives the programmer a description of the variable.
The variable declaration int answer; /* the result of our expression */ defines a variable named answer of type int.
An integer is a whole number with no fractional part or decimal point.
The limits on integers are implementation dependent, meaning they change from computer to computer.
The assignment statement answer = (1 + 2) * 4; assigns the value of the expression (1 + 2) * 4 to the variable answer.
The general form of the assignment statement is: variable = expression;
In C, the = operator is used for assignment, not for testing equality.
The printf function is a library function used to print the results.
The printf statement "printf("Twice %d is %d\n", term, 2 * term);" prints the value of term and twice its value.
The general form of the printf statement is: printf(format, expression-1, expression-2, ...);
The %d conversion in the format string is called the integer conversion specification.
A floating-point number is a real number stored internally in a specific format.
The numbers 5.5, 8.3, and -12.6 are all floating-point numbers.
The form of a floating-point declaration is: float variable;
The %f conversion is used to print a floating-point number using printf.
In an integer divide, the result is truncated, whereas in a floating-point divide, the result is a floating-point number.
The expression 19 / 10 is an integer divide and results in 1, whereas 19.0 / 10.0 is a floating-point divide and results in 1.9.
C allows the assignment of an integer expression to a floating-point variable and performs the conversion automatically.
The type char represents single characters.
Characters are enclosed in single quotes.
The backslash character (\) is called the escape character.
The characters \" can be used to put a double quote inside a string.
A single quote is represented by \'.
\n is the newline character.
Characters can be specified by \nnn, where nnn is the octal code for the character.
A string is a different data type that is enclosed in double quotes.
Strings can have any number of characters.
Characters use the printf conversion %c.
Example 4-7 reverses three characters using the code: printf("%c%c%c reversed is %c%c%c\n", char1, char2, char3, char3, char2, char1);
In C, the index of an array starts at 0.
The dimension of an array must be specified when it is declared.
The number of elements in an array is called the dimension of the array.
Example 5-1 computes the total and average of five numbers using an array.
To reference an element of an array, you use a number called the index.
Strings are sequences of characters.
C does not have a built-in string type; instead, strings are created out of character arrays.
The special character '\0' (NUL) is used to indicate the end of a string.

fgets includes the end-of-line in the string.

Clearly identify all error messages with the word 'error,' and try to give the user some idea of how to correct his problem.
Put one variable declaration per line, and comment them.
Make variable-names long enough to be easily understood, but not so long that they are difficult to type in.
Never use default declarations. If a function returns an integer, declare it as type int.
All parameters to a function should be declared and commented. Never use default declarations.
int main(void) /* Correct declaration */
Always put a default case in a switch statement. Even if the case does nothing, put it in.
Every case in a switch should end with a break or /* Fall through */ comment.
Always put parentheses around each constant expression defined by a preprocessor #define directive.
Use const declarations instead of #define wherever possible.
Put ( ) around each argument of a parameterized macro.
#define SQUARE(x) ((x) * (x))
Surround macros that contain complete statements with curly braces ({ }).
#define DIE(msg) {printf(msg);exit(8);}
When using the #ifdef/#endif construct for conditional compilation, put the #define and #undef statements near the top of the program and comment them.
A single block of code enclosed in {} should not span more than a couple of pages. Anything much bigger than that should probably be split up into several smaller, simpler procedures.
Always create a Makefile so that others will know how to compile your program.
Turn on all the warning flags, then make your program warning free.
The statement (number =! 2) is not a relational equation, but an assignment statement.
if (number =! 2) /* incorrect */
